Event: Catelyn pleads with Walder Frey to spare Robb and offers herself in his place
Explanation: She gains nothing and is killed soon after, but the attempt is meant to help Robb and causes no added harm to others.

Event: Catelyn attempts to free and exchange Jaime Lannister for her daughters
Explanation: Risking her and Robb’s position and their main leverage for a very uncertain exchange harms her side more than any clear benefit to others.

Event: Catelyn prays for the gods to take baby Jon away
Explanation: She wishes lethal harm on an innocent child out of resentment, with no benefit to herself and potential immense harm to Jon.

Event: Catelyn prays for Jon to be saved and promises to love and legitimize him
Explanation: She seeks Jon’s survival and his full acceptance, improving his prospects and easing her guilt, benefiting both.

Event: Catelyn refuses to fulfill her promise to love and legitimize Jon after he recovers
Explanation: She continues to harm Jon emotionally and believes this brings divine punishment on her family, hurting both sides.

Event: Catelyn tells Ned to trust Littlefinger and vouches for him
Explanation: Her misplaced trust directly abets Ned’s downfall while giving her no real long-term gain and enabling catastrophic harm.

Event: Catelyn vows revenge on the Lannisters after Ned’s death
Explanation: Her resolve may bolster her own sense of purpose while committing to further violence and destruction against others.

Event: Catelyn glares Jon Snow while he says goodbye to comatose Bran
Explanation: Her hostility yields no benefit to her and inflicts emotional harm on Jon at a vulnerable moment.

Event: Catelyn locks herself away and refuses to eat for days after Brandon’s death
Explanation: Her self-starving and withdrawal damage only herself and do not clearly hurt others.

Event: Catelyn saves Littlefinger from Brandon Stark years earlier
Explanation: She spares Littlefinger out of pity, helping him survive while enabling someone who will later devastate her family and herself.

Event: Catelyn threatens to slit a hostage’s throat when someone tries to murder her son
Explanation: She credibly threatens the hostage to deter harm and protect her child, gaining leverage and enhancing her side’s safety.

Event: Catelyn fights the assassin attacking Bran, grabbing the dagger and biting his hands
Explanation: She risks injury to delay the killer, directly contributing to Bran’s survival and proving her own courage and value.

Event: Catelyn prays for Jon to die of illness when he is a baby
Explanation: Again she tries to secure the death of an innocent infant without any rational self-benefit, maximizing harm to him.

Event: Catelyn makes a Bargain with Heaven to save Jon, promising future love and legitimization
Explanation: She tries to secure Jon’s life and a better future for him while alleviating her own guilt and potential divine wrath.

Event: Catelyn refuses to uphold her promise after Jon survives
Explanation: Her broken vow perpetuates Jon’s marginalization and fuels her belief she has invited tragedy onto herself.

Event: Catelyn captures and has Tyrion arrested on Littlefinger’s word
Explanation: She acts on flimsy evidence to gain supposed leverage for her family, but this sparks a destructive war harming many others.

Event: Catelyn kidnaps Tyrion by taking him to the Eyrie
Explanation: She deepens the unlawful seizure to strengthen her bargaining power, provoking Tywin and worsening looming conflict.

Event: Catelyn reams out Robb for sending Theon to bargain with Balon
Explanation: Her criticism aims to protect Robb from Greyjoy treachery, supporting his leadership and their cause.

Event: Catelyn ignores Ned’s caution and expects him to trust Littlefinger
Explanation: Her insistence on trusting Petyr undermines Ned’s judgment and contributes to his downfall without benefiting her.

Event: Catelyn arrests Tyrion publicly in the inn with her father’s bannermen
Explanation: She leverages surprise and politics to seize him as a bargaining chip, boosting perceived Stark strength but pushing toward war.

Event: Catelyn releases Jaime Lannister to trade for Sansa and Arya
Explanation: She sacrifices their prime hostage on slim odds of a fair exchange, weakening Robb’s position and not clearly helping the girls.

Event: Catelyn calls Robb’s breaking his Frey betrothal to marry Talisa monumentally stupid
Explanation: Her rebuke is aimed at preventing political disaster for Robb and their house, protecting both her and their followers.

Event: Catelyn makes plain she disapproves of Robb marrying Talisa
Explanation: She tries to stop a match that breaks an essential alliance, safeguarding Robb’s kingship and Frey relations.

Event: Catelyn gradually warms up to Talisa and accepts her as Robb’s wife
Explanation: Her acceptance strengthens family unity and emotional support without evident strategic cost at that stage.

Event: Catelyn calmly but fiercely calls on her father’s bannermen to seize Tyrion
Explanation: She orchestrates a show of legalistic power to detain him, increasing her leverage while escalating danger to others.

Event: Catelyn regrets refusing to love Jon and believes the gods punished her family
Explanation: Her retrospective guilt torments her psychologically without aiding anyone else.

Event: Catelyn blames herself for her family’s misfortunes due to breaking her vow about Jon
Explanation: Self-recrimination increases her suffering and provides no clear benefit to others.

Event: Catelyn treats Jon Snow coldly and makes it clear she doesn’t want him around
Explanation: Her ongoing rejection harms Jon’s well-being and yields no gain for her, even straining household harmony.

Event: Catelyn almost smashes Jaime’s head with a rock while mourning Ned
Explanation: Had she continued, killing a key hostage might briefly satisfy vengeance but severely hurt her side’s bargaining position.

Event: Catelyn calms herself instead of killing Jaime with the rock
Explanation: By restraining herself she preserves a crucial bargaining chip and avoids rash action, helping both her cause and Jaime’s life.

Event: Catelyn warns Robb about crossing the Greyjoys and Freys
Explanation: Her accurate strategic warning aims to protect Robb and their forces from betrayal.

Event: Catelyn trusts Lysa’s letter blaming the Lannisters and acts on it
Explanation: Her credulity toward Lysa’s claims helps propel the Stark–Lannister conflict without securing proportional benefits.

Event: Catelyn trusts Lysa to give Tyrion a fair trial
Explanation: She misjudges Lysa’s instability, exposing Tyrion to a sham process while not meaningfully aiding herself.

Event: Catelyn resolves, after Ned’s death, that “we will kill them all”
Explanation: Her dedication to total vengeance may bolster her purpose but steers toward mass harm with little chance of positive resolution.

Event: Catelyn stops Robb from laughing at Edmure’s failure with a pointed glare
Explanation: She maintains decorum and spares Edmure humiliation while reinforcing Robb’s maturity as a leader.

Event: Catelyn tries to give Jaime a “Kingslayer” moral lecture
Explanation: Though verbally parried, her attempt to morally confront him seeks understanding and possibly future restraint without clear harm.
